data.title: ICE Canola Rising in Early Trading
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--ICE canola futures were posting solid gains Friday morning, seeing a continuation of Thursday's correction off five-month lows.

China's Ministry of Commerce said Friday it would prolong its anti-dumping probe into Canadian canola for six months, with a final ruling now not expected until March 9, 2026. The extension raised some optimism that a deal could be reached, although preliminary Chinese duties of 75.8% imposed on Canadian canola seed imports in August remain in place. A Canadian trade delegation, including Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe and Prime Minister Mark Carney's parliamentary secretary Kody Blois, is set to travel to China this weekend.

Chicago soybeans and European rapeseed futures were higher, providing some underlying support. However, soyoil was softer and the Malaysian palm oil market was closed for a holiday.

Seasonal harvest pressure amid a lack of significant weather concerns remained a bearish influence in the background.

About 24,100 canola contracts had traded as of 9:48 a.m. EDT.

data.title: CONSUMERS BANCORP INC /OH/ SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Consumers Bancorp, Inc., a community-oriented financial institution and bank holding company for Consumers National Bank, has released its annual Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges and risks it faces in the highly regulated banking industry.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Interest and Dividend Income: $53.05 million, reflecting an increase due to higher yields on interest-earning assets.
  • Net Interest Income: $34.11 million, up from $31.99 million in the previous year, driven by an increase in the yield on average interest-earning assets.
  • Net Income: $8.67 million, a slight increase from $8.58 million in the prior year, supported by higher net interest income and noninterest income.
  • Basic and Diluted Earnings Per Share: $2.77, consistent with the previous year's $2.76, indicating stable earnings performance.
  • Total Noninterest Income: $5.45 million, an increase of 11.3% from the prior year, primarily due to higher debit card interchange income and bank-owned life insurance income.
  • Total Noninterest Expenses: $28.09 million, an increase of 8.7% from the previous year, mainly due to higher salaries, occupancy, and equipment expenses.

Business Highlights

  • Company Overview: Consumers Bancorp, Inc. is a bank holding company that primarily holds the common stock of Consumers National Bank. The Bank is a community-oriented financial institution offering a range of commercial and consumer loan and deposit products, as well as mortgage, financial planning, and investment services.
  • Market Area: The Bank operates primarily in Carroll, Columbiana, Jefferson, Mahoning, Stark, and Summit counties in Ohio, with additional markets in northeast Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.
  • Branch Network: As of June 30, 2025, the Bank had 22 full-service branch locations and one loan production office, indicating a strong physical presence in its market area.
  • Loan Portfolio: The Bank's loan portfolio includes commercial & industrial loans, commercial real estate loans, farmland loans, land development loans, 1-4 family residential real estate loans, and consumer loans. The portfolio saw a 7.2% increase in loan receivables to $813,458 as of June 30, 2025.
  • Consumer Loans Growth: There was a significant increase in consumer loans by 54.5%, attributed to the expansion of indirect auto lending within the Bank's market areas.
  • Non-Performing Loans: Non-performing loans were 0.11% of total loans as of June 30, 2025, reflecting a slight increase from the previous year, with management actively monitoring these loans.
  • Allowance for Credit Losses: The allowance for credit losses as a percentage of total loans remained stable at 1.04%, indicating consistent management of credit risk.
  • Deposit Growth: Total deposits increased by 6.6% to $1.04 billion, with a favorable funding mix that includes a significant portion of noninterest-bearing demand deposits.
  • Liquidity Management: The Bank maintains a strong liquidity position with significant sources of funds, including deposits, lines of credit, and access to the Federal Home Loan Bank of Cincinnati.
  • Cybersecurity and Compliance: The Bank is actively engaged in maintaining robust cybersecurity measures and compliance with regulatory requirements, including recent updates from the SEC and federal banking regulators.
  • Future Outlook: Management anticipates continued growth in the Bank's market areas, with a focus on maintaining strong asset quality and capital adequacy while navigating economic and regulatory challenges.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: Consumers Bancorp, Inc. has focused on maintaining a strong liquidity position by increasing total assets to $1.17 billion, primarily through a $54.3 million increase in total loans funded by a $63.8 million increase in total deposits. The company has also expanded its indirect auto lending within its market areas, contributing to a significant increase in consumer loans.
  • Capital Management: The company increased its total shareholders’ equity by $12.6 million, driven by net income and a decrease in accumulated other comprehensive loss. It paid $2.4 million in cash dividends and maintained a dividend payout ratio of 27.5%. The company also issued shares through stock-based incentive plans and a dividend reinvestment plan, contributing to the increase in common shares. Additionally, the company has maintained a favorable funding mix with a significant portion of deposits in noninterest-bearing demand deposits.
  • Future Outlook: Consumers Bancorp, Inc. plans to continue leveraging its strong liquidity position to support growth initiatives, including potential increases in the residential mortgage warehouse line-of-credit as mortgage volumes rise. The company also intends to maintain its focus on community banking, which has resulted in a favorable deposit mix and lower cost of funds. The company expects to manage its capital resources effectively to support ongoing growth and shareholder returns.

Challenges and Risks

  • Regulatory Environment: Consumers Bancorp, Inc. operates in a highly regulated environment, subject to oversight by multiple federal and state regulatory bodies, including the SEC, Federal Reserve Board, and OCC. Changes in laws or regulatory policies could materially impact the company's business and prospects.
  • Economic Risks: Changes in local, regional, and national economic conditions could lead to a deterioration in asset credit quality, impacting debtors' ability to meet obligations due to high unemployment rates and inflationary pressures.
  • Interest Rate Risks: Rapid fluctuations in market interest rates could affect fair market valuations and lead to a decline in net interest income.
  • Regulatory Risks: Changes in trade, monetary, and fiscal policies, including interest rate policies of the Federal Reserve Board, could affect the company's operations.
  • Liquidity Risks: Unanticipated changes in liquidity position, including the cost of liquidity and the ability to find alternative funding sources, could pose challenges.
  • Competitive Risks: Increased competitive pressures on product pricing and services could impact the company's market position.
  • Cybersecurity Risks: Breaches of security or failures of technology systems due to technological factors and cybersecurity threats could adversely affect operations.
  • Operational Risks: Changes in consumer spending, borrowing, and savings habits, as well as declining asset values impacting collateral, could affect the company's financial stability.
  • Human Resource Risks: The ability to attract and retain qualified employees is crucial for maintaining operational efficiency.

SEC Filing: CONSUMERS BANCORP INC /OH/ [ CBKM ] - 10-K - Sep. 05, 2025

data.title: Canada's Ivey PMI falls to three-month low in August
data.description:
Canadian economic activity barely expanded in August as employment declined and prices cooled, Ivey Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) data showed on Friday.

The seasonally adjusted index fell to 50.1 last month from 55.8 in July, posting its lowest level since May.

The Ivey PMI measures the month-to-month variation in economic activity as indicated by a panel of purchasing managers from across Canada. A reading above 50 indicates an increase in activity.

The gauge of employment dropped to an adjusted 46.0 from 51.2 in July, while the prices index was at 65.1, down from 68.5.

The unadjusted PMI fell to 50.0 from 54.6.

data.title: US natural gas prices hold near one-month high on lower output
data.description:
U.S. natural gas futures firmed and held near a one-month high on Friday, aided by a drop in output in recent weeks and forecasts for slightly more demand in the coming weeks.

Front-month gas futures for October delivery on the New York Mercantile Exchange rose 3.7 cents, or 1.2%, to $3.11 per million British thermal units at 09:29 a.m. EDT (1329 GMT), hovering near Wednesday's peak, when the contract hit its highest level since August 7. The contract was on track for a second straight weekly gain, up 4.2% for the week so far.

Financial firm LSEG said average gas output in the Lower 48 states has fallen to 107.5 billion cubic feet per day so far in September, down from a record monthly high of 108.3 bcfd in August.

LSEG projected average gas demand in the Lower 48 states, including exports, would rise from 102.3 bcfd in the current week to 103.7 bcfd next week.

"Natural gas has found new footing but any rally for the October contract will likely face stiff resistance around $3.18, while support at $3.04 should hold us away from any deep reversal," said Gary Cunningham, director of market research at Tradition Energy.

"Some mid-month warmth in the heartlands could give us a little boost, but with population centers in the east mostly shielded from any late-season power demands for cooling we should be somewhat limited in overall gas consumption through September."

Meteorologists forecast the weather will remain mostly near normal through September 19, consistent with previous expectations.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) on Thursday said energy firms injected 55 billion cubic feet (bcf) of gas into storage during the week ended August 29. That was close to the 56-bcf build analysts forecast in a Reuters poll and compares with an increase of 16 bcf during the same week last year and an average build of 36 bcf over the past five years.

Elsewhere, Dutch and British gas prices were little changed on Friday morning, continuing to trade in a tight range in the face of a scarcity of news with the potential to affect the market and no changes to the wider supply and demand balance.

data.title: What Being an 80-Year-Old for a Day Teaches You About Aging — WSJ
data.description:
By Conor Grant

This is an edition of The Future of Everything newsletter, a look at how innovation and technology are transforming the way we live, work and play. If you're not subscribed, sign up here .

What does it feel like to be a woman in her 80s?

To answer that question, Amy Dockser Marcus turned to Agnes, which is short for "Age Gain Now Empathy System," an age-simulation suit made by MIT AgeLab. This week, the health and science reporter shares her experience trying out the simulated older body — and what it taught her about how all of us can prepare to grow older.

The original MIT AgeLab suit was conceived as part of a training exercise for engineers, designers and marketers at a German automaker looking at what older customers might want in luxury cars. Since then, Agnes has been refined to replicate the experience of people in their late 70s to early 80s who struggle with several unmanaged conditions.

The suit consists of weights worn around the torso, ankles and wrists that replicate the loss of muscle mass, a bungee cord system that limits mobility, a padded collar that limits neck rotation, goggles that distort vision and foam-padded Crocs that complicate balance.

Completing routine daily tasks while wearing the suit posed a number of challenges, reports Marcus. The suit made it difficult to reach grocery products on high shelves, navigate a self-checkout pad, ride the subway, open a can of soup and cross the street. And it was tiring — both physically and mentally.

More on this topic:

  • Essay: Americans in their 80s and 90s are redefining old age. (Read)
  • One doctor's science-backed formula for aging better. (Read)
  • 🎧 The longevity business is booming, but is there a limit to our lifespan? (Listen)

🤔 What do you see as the most difficult hurdles in aging? How, if at all, are you preparing for them? Send me your thoughts, questions and predictions at future@wsj.com (if you're reading this in your inbox, you can just hit reply).

More of What's Next: Drone Swarms in Battle; Space Habitats; AI Literacy

AI-powered drone swarms have now entered the battlefield. Ukraine is using artificial intelligence to allow groups of drones to communicate and make decisions independent of their operator in combat, marking a new frontier for warfare.

Space habitats are getting an extreme makeover. Space architects are designing living quarters that promise more room, light and color, while also protecting occupants from the dangers of radiation and orbiting debris.

The less you know about AI, the more likely you are to use it. New research suggests AI can seem magical to those with low AI literacy, making them more willing to try it. The finding challenges the assumption that more technical knowledge will naturally lead to wider AI adoption.

Apple's Vision Pro is gaining traction in some niches of business, writes Steven Rosenbush. Though it's too pricey for most consumers, the $3,500 mixed-reality headset may have found a calling in narrow and deep business applications such as helping train pilots or designing kitchens.

Future Feedback

Last week, we reported on efforts to reimagine trash pickup. Readers shared their thoughts on these potential updates to trash collection:

  • "My wife and I lived in Japan from 2002 to 2003. We were blown away by the amount of sorting and recycling we were required to do. (We even had our trash returned to us for not sorting 'burnables' and 'recyclables' appropriately.) When we returned to the U.S. we felt so wasteful throwing everything into one bin, which was the only curbside option we had." -- Steve Gwinnup, Michigan
  • "Self-driving garbage cans seem a bit over the top -- one more thing to plug in. But I like WALL-E...." -- Gil Schrage, Kentucky
  • "As a commercial property manager in San Francisco back in the aughts, I learned that our trash was billed by weight -- but that pickup of recyclables was free as long as we didn't contaminate our waste streams. By working with tenants, we reduced the complexity and ended up saving 40% on our monthly trash bill! It was a win-win-win for everyone!" -- April Capil, California
  • "I would love to have automated waste cans that will transport themselves up and down my steep driveway. We have single-stream recycling, which means two cans to manipulate each week -- which is more difficult for seniors to handle." -- Paula Grazzini, Virginia

(Responses have been condensed and edited.)

Elsewhere in the Future

  • Therapists are secretly using ChatGPT. Clients are triggered. (MIT Technology Review)
  • How "clanker" became an anti-AI rallying cry. (The New York Times)
  • BMW plans to unveil "superbrain" EVs to challenge Chinese rivals. (Financial Times)

About Us

Thanks for reading The Future of Everything. We cover the innovation and tech transforming the way we live, work and play. This newsletter was written by Conor Grant. Get in touch with us at future@wsj.com.

See more from The Future of Everything at wsj.com/future-of-everything.

data.title: JOHN WILEY & SONS, INC. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
John Wiley & Sons, Inc., a global leader in research and education, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter. The report highlights the company's financial performance, operational achievements, and strategic initiatives aimed at driving future growth and efficiency.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $396.8 million, decreased by 2% compared with the prior year due to divested businesses.
  • Operating Income: $31.0 million, increased by 7% compared with the prior year, primarily due to lower operating and administrative expenses.
  • Net Income: $11.7 million, compared to a net loss of $1.4 million in the prior year, reflecting improved profitability.
  • Diluted EPS: $0.22, increased by $0.25 per share compared with the prior year diluted loss per share, driven by a decrease in the income tax provision and an increase in operating income.

Business Highlights

  • Research Segment Performance: The Research segment reported a 6% increase in revenue, driven by a 45% growth in Research Solutions, which includes AI license revenue. Research Publishing remained stable with a slight decrease of 1% on a constant currency basis.
  • Learning Segment Performance: The Learning segment experienced a decline in revenue, with Academic products generating $55.5 million and Professional products generating $59.6 million. The segment faced challenges due to lower employee costs and reduced bad debt expenses.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates annualized cost savings of approximately $105 million from the Global Restructuring Program, with most savings expected to be realized in fiscal year 2026. The focus will be on optimizing cost structures and aligning technology costs.
  • Operational Efficiency: The Global Restructuring Program aims to enhance operational efficiency by reducing real estate occupancy by 35% and streamlining corporate expenses.
  • AI License Revenue: AI license revenue significantly contributed to the Research Solutions growth, with $28.9 million reported for the quarter, including $15.7 million from content licensed from other publishers.

SEC Filing: JOHN WILEY & SONS, INC. [ WLY ] - 10-Q - Sep. 05, 2025

data.title: HubSpot Anticipated AI's Hit to Web Traffic — Market Talk
data.description: Investors have expressed concern that Google AI Overviews will hurt HubSpot by taking away valuable web traffic. But the company anticipated this change years in advance and pivoted beyond search to alternate emerging channels including YouTube and podcasts, say JPMorgan analysts. HubSpot is now cited in more large language models than any other customer relationship management company and is now tracking when AI generates leads for customers' businesses, which will help the company sustain demand in the AI era. "HubSpot's adept maneuvering should be viewed as a feather in its cap and an ability to see around corners," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Lululemon Stock Tanks on Yet Another Guidance Cut. 'It's Going to Get Worse,' Says Analyst. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Sabrina Escobar and Elsa Ohlen

Lululemon Athletica handily topped second-quarter earnings estimates, but the stock tanked on a narrow sales miss and the company's second consecutive cut to full-year guidance.

"It's going to get worse ... much worse," said Jefferies analyst Randal Konik.

Lululemon has been pressured by tariffs as well as weakness in its key U.S. market. The company cut its full-year guidance for the second time in three months late Thursday and now expects net revenue to range from $10.85 billion to $11 billion, down from previous guidance for $11.15 billion to $11.30 billion, and below consensus of $11.2 billion at the midpoint.

"The guide is not low enough," Konik said, reiterating an Underperform rating on the shares with a $150 price target, citing rising competition, lower growth, and fading brand strength.

Lululemon stock plummeted 18% to $169.13 in premarket trading. Coming into the session, shares have shed 46% this year.

The company also revised its earnings guidance lower to reflect both the slowdown in sales and bigger-than-expected tariff impacts. Earnings per share will now range from $12.77 to $12.97 for the year, compared with a prior forecast of $14.58 to $14.78. Analysts were forecasting earnings of $14.40 for the year.

The guidance includes an estimated reduction in gross profit of about $240 million related to changes in trade policy, including higher tariffs and the removal of the de minimis exemption, Lululemon said.

While some near-term headwinds had been expected, the scrapping of the de minimis tariff exception is a far bigger issue than anticipated and puts additional pressure on margins, noted Evercore's Michael Binetti. The analyst downgraded the stock to In Line from Outperform and lowered the price target to $180 from $265.

Lululemon's second-quarter earnings of $3.10 a share came in ahead of the consensus estimates for $2.86 among analysts tracked by FactSet.

Revenue rose 7% year over year to $2.53 billion, narrowly missing analysts' projections for $2.54 billion, according to FactSet. Same-store sales rose 1% year over year, well below Wall Street expectations for a 3.7% increase.

The lackluster performance stems from a 4% same-store sales decline in Lululemon's Americas business — the company's most important source of revenue. Demand in North America has been struggling for several quarters now, a result of both slowing trends in the broader athleisure industry and ongoing market share gains among Lululemon's competitors.

"While we continued to see positive momentum overall in our international regions in the second quarter, we are disappointed with our U.S. business results and aspects of our product execution," said CEO Calvin McDonald in a press release. "We have closely assessed the drivers of our underperformance and are continuing to take the necessary actions to strengthen our merchandise mix and accelerate our business."

Performance was better in other countries, with same-store sales rising 17% year over year in China and 12% in the rest of the world. That momentum, however, wasn't enough to make up for the lost revenue in the U.S.

"Lululemon's international franchise is saving the show, but the core U.S. business has lost its rhythm," wrote David Bartosiak, stock strategist at Zacks Investment Research, in emailed comments to Barron's. "Investors wanted a sun salutation, but what they got was more of a downward dog. Now it's all about whether this brand can bend without breaking under tariff pressure and home-market weakness."

Write to Sabrina Escobar at sabrina.escobar@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ancient Teeth Reveal New Species of Human Ancestor — WSJ
data.description:
By Aylin Woodward

A handful of teeth unearthed in Ethiopia has led to the discovery of a new species of human ancestor.

The finding, announced in August in the journal Nature, began with a Valentine's Day trek seven years ago. Paleoanthropologists were hunting for fossils in the country's Afar region when an Ethiopian field assistant, Omar Abdulla Omar, suddenly stooped down.

He had spotted a tooth.

Later that afternoon, Omar dropped to his knees again, finding another molar in the volcanic soil. Over the next week, the team found a total of nine molars and incisors across the Afar archaeological site known as Ledi-Geraru, all dating to around 2.6 million years ago.

"It's almost like a miracle every time we find that one of our ancestors died in the right place at the right time to be fossilized for us to find three million years later," said Amy Rector, an anthropologist at Virginia Commonwealth University and co-author of the study describing the team's discovery.

The era between two million and three million years ago is critical for scientific study, Rector said, because it is when the earliest members of our human lineage, the genus Homo, first evolved and when Australopithecus, another early human primate group, disappeared.

The Australopithecus lineage included early humans like "Lucy," famous for being a member of one of the first species to habitually walk on two legs.

The researchers had previously uncovered four other teeth at the site, but found that the two sets came from different species: the four belonged to an early Homo lineage, and the other nine to a hereto undiscovered Australopithecus.

For years, the researchers laboriously compared the teeth to those from other early human lineages living in eastern Africa around this time. The nine differed significantly in size, structure and shape from other known species' teeth.

"I think the team did a great job of demonstrating that the dental morphology in the Australopithecus specimens is distinct from the other species currently known," said Kevin Hatala, a paleoanthropologist at Chatham University in Pennsylvania who wasn't involved in the work.

The finding suggests that multiple early human species coexisted in this part of the continent several million years ago — a conclusion that runs contrary to what many paleoanthropologists once thought happened in ancient Africa.

In the 1990s and 2000s, many experts thought the Homo lineage evolved directly from the Australopithecus lineage, according to Kaye Reed, a paleoanthropologist at Arizona State University's Institute of Human Origins and co-author of the recent study.

But "evolution doesn't work that way," she said. "It wasn't a solo ancestor walking through time."

Rather, a growing body of evidence suggests species from multiple lineages overlapped in space and time and adapted to fill specific ecological niches.

The researchers have yet to give a moniker to the new species.

"You can't name something based on, you know, teeth," Reed said. "We need a skull!"

She and her colleagues hope to find additional fossil evidence in subsequent field missions.

Write to Aylin Woodward at aylin.woodward@wsj.com
